medications.indication_rationale: Regular high-dose non-sedating antihistamine to suppress histamine-driven wheals (up-titrate as needed).
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., 20 mg stat then up to twice daily for up to ~14 days (as per source note; per guidance)

medications.indication_rationale: Likely trigger — cease and avoid NSAIDs; use paracetamol if analgesia needed.
For any airway/breathing/circulatory features — use the auto-injector (if available) and call 000. Not needed for skin-only urticaria.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the antihistamine regularly (not just when itchy), avoid all NSAIDs, wear loose cotton clothing, take cool showers/use cool compresses, and monitor breathing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Suppresses recurrence, removes the trigger, and enables early detection of anaphylaxis.

patient_education_points.point: These are hives — your skin releasing histamine, most likely triggered by the new ibuprofen. It's confined to your skin and not dangerous by itself. The antihistamines block it; take them regularly even when clear.
Stop the ibuprofen and avoid all anti-inflammatory painkillers; use paracetamol instead. Hives may come and go for a few weeks.
If you get any swelling of your lips/tongue/throat, trouble breathing or wheezing, or feel faint/dizzy or start vomiting — use an adrenaline pen if you have one and call 000 immediately.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; discuss triggers/allergy work-up if it becomes chronic.

must_include_items: Screen for anaphylaxis/angioedema FIRST and diagnose skin-only acute urticaria
Identify and cease the likely NSAID trigger
acceptable_alternatives: Short oral steroid for severe refractory urticaria per guidance
errors_of_commission: Prescribing adrenaline for skin-only urticaria as routine treatment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires an anaphylaxis screen + safety-net, high-dose antihistamine, and NSAID cessation. Missing the anaphylaxis safety-net is the key failure.
